channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How Trump and His Advisers Miscalculated Iran’s Response to War
item.description: In the lead-up to the U.S.-Israeli attack, President Trump downplayed the risks to the energy markets as a short-term concern that should not overshadow the mission to decapitate the Iranian regime.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti, Tyler Pager and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: In response to Iranian threats, commercial shipping has come to a standstill in the Gulf, and oil prices have spiked.

item.title: Has the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement Affected You? We Want to Hear About It.
item.description: The New York Times is looking to talk to people about the impact that the trade deal between the United States, Canada and Mexico has had on them and their businesses.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson

item.title: How the Iran War is Affecting Aluminum, Helium, Sulfur and More
item.description: Prices of some commodities are already rising, while stockpiles of others could dwindle as the war drags on.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Ras Laffan Industrial City in Qatar. Production of helium has been disrupted since Iran attacked the natural gas hub.

item.title: Senate Moves Toward Passing Sweeping Housing Bill, but Challenges Lie Ahead
item.description: The legislation’s progress is all the more surprising because it addresses an issue that is shaping up to be the main battleground of the midterm elections: affordability. The effort could still stall.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction on a home in Simpsonville, S.C., in January.

item.title: Boeing Says Wiring Issue Will Delay Some 737 Max Deliveries
item.description: The aerospace company said the delay would not prevent it from meeting its 2026 sales goal of about 500 Max jets.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: Boeing 737 Max Groundings and Safety Concerns (2019)
item.media:description: A Boeing 737 Max at the company’s plant in Renton, Wash. Boeing said on Tuesday that it would delay delivery of some 737 Max jets as wiring issues were fixed.

item.title: Higher Oil Prices Could Put the Fed in a Bind as Labor Market Softens
item.description: Investors now expect that the Fed will delay a rate cut until September instead of July, as they had before the war in Iran began.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Economists expect energy-related price pressures to push inflation up to some degree in the coming months.

item.title: What to Know About the Rising Price of Oil and Its Effects on Airline Ticket Prices
item.description: The Gulf conflict has led to higher fuel prices, and the cost of operating a plane has sharply increased. For travelers, that likely means higher fares.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Glusac
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: How much the cost of an airline ticket may rise is unclear. The last time oil rose more than 40 percent — in 2022, on the heels of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine — airfares jumped 28 percent.

item.title: YouTube Adds Tool to Help Public Figures Report Fake Videos
item.description: Social media companies are under pressure to crack down on so-called deepfake videos that use deceptive images of real people.
item.dc:creator: Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Anastasiia Sapon for The New York Times
item.media:description: The San Bruno, Calif., headquarters of YouTube, which is introducing a program to help participants monitor and report videos that use artificial intelligence to impersonate them.

item.title: Rising Fuel Costs Pressure Airlines and Truckers
item.description: The price of jet fuel and diesel has surged since the war in Iran began, which could force airlines and trucking companies to pass on higher costs to their customers.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi, Emmett Lindner and Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The cost of jet fuel has reached record highs since the U.S.-led war with Iran began.

item.title: USA Today Names Jamie Stockwell as Next Top Editor
item.description: Ms. Stockwell, who recently left The Washington Post, is the third person named to the job in the past three years.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson

item.title: Businesses Ask the TACO Question About Iran
item.description: Whether President Trump signaled that he may soon end the military strikes is weighing on the minds of investors, executives and others.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s conflicting signals about ending the strikes on Iran have kept investors on edge.

item.title: San Francisco Bay Area Residents Weigh Possibility of BART Reductions
item.description: The Bay Area Rapid Transit system was once so successful, it could rely mostly on riders to sustain itself. But the pandemic dealt BART an unusually heavy blow.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight and Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Minh Connors for The New York Times
item.media:description: BART directors say that Bay Area residents can rescue the system by passing a new sales tax in November.

item.title: China Stockpiled More Oil Before War Broke Out in Iran
item.description: China’s oil imports rose nearly 16 percent in the first two months of 2026, adding to its growing energy stockpile.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil tanker unloading crude oil at a terminal at the port in Qingdao, in China’s eastern Shandong province, on Tuesday

item.title: How to Create Your Own Affordable Wellness Vacation
item.description: You don’t need to spend a fortune to participate in one of travel’s hottest trends. Here are some suggestions, including seaweed soaks, robotic massages, sound baths and sauna circuits.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Glusac

item.title: Oil Falls and Stocks Steady After a Day of Drastic Swings
item.description: A day after the price of oil had spiked to nearly $120 a barrel, the S&P 500 ended Tuesday 0.2 percent lower, even as the U.S. bombing campaign of Iran intensified.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison, Rich Barbieri and Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: America Is an Oil Exporter. Why Does a Mideast War Raise U.S. Gas Prices?
item.description: The U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran have intensified and the conflict has widened, shaking global energy markets.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: F.A.A. Briefly Halts JetBlue Departures After System Outage
item.description: The Federal Aviation Administration issued a ground stop for JetBlue flights early Tuesday at the airline’s request. JetBlue later said a “system outage” had been restored and that operations had resumed.
item.dc:creator: Dan Watson

item.title: Fed Up With High Costs, American Theater Takes a Trip to London
item.description: Skyrocketing budgets and falling profitability have driven a new form of offshoring, with U.S. producers staging shows across the Atlantic.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Joshua Atkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Hunter as F. Scott Fitzgerald in a rehearsal for the new American musical “Beautiful Little Fool,” which had an initial run this winter at the Southwark Playhouse in London.

item.title: U.S. Solar Installations Fell in 2025 as Trump Attacked Clean Energy
item.description: More solar energy was added to U.S. grids than any other technology, but the amount installed fell by 14 percent, according to a new report.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.media:credit: Mason Trinca for The New York Times
item.media:description: A solar field in Riesel, Texas, in 2023. Solar power is projected to account for just over half of the new power projects that will be added this year.

item.title: Fashionable Wall Street Bros Set Off a Social Media Storm
item.description: They said yes to an Interview magazine fashion shoot. Then came the haters.
item.dc:creator: Guy Trebay
item.media:description: A fierce online reaction greeted a pictorial featuring junior bankers in high-end suits.

item.title: How the Iran War Could Hit Americans’ Wallets
item.description: Gas prices are rising, with the cost of food likely to follow. If the conflict drags on, the fallout for consumers could worsen, experts warned.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Rising oil and gas prices could lead to modest increases in food prices, experts said,

item.title: Karen Petrou, Influential Fiscal Policy Analyst and Critic, Dies at 72
item.description: Her insights on financial regulations and monetary policy guided big banks and Washington policymakers.
item.dc:creator: Stacy Cowley
item.media:credit: Sarah Silbiger for The New York Times
item.media:description: Karen Petrou, with a guide dog, Ike, in 2021 at her home in Washington, D.C. She took to using guide dogs in recent decades when her eyesight deteriorated — an alliance she often invoked in her writing.

item.title: F.D.A. Opens Door to More Flavored E-Cigarettes
item.description: The agency is proposing allowing other tastes for vapes, like mint, coffees, spices or tea, to appeal to adults. At the same time, illicit candy-flavored types aimed at teenagers have flooded the market.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The F.D.A. said it would consider vapes in flavors such as mint, coffees, teas and spices like cinnamon, but would continue to reject those offering sweet or fruity flavors.

item.title: U.S. Reaches Tentative Deal Ending Prosecution of Turkish Bank
item.description: Court papers submitted as part of the agreement with Halkbank, which was accused of doing business with Iranian entities, said Turkey’s assistance “was instrumental” in the Israel-Hamas cease-fire.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:description: President Recep Tayyip Erdogan of Turkey has long pressed President Trump to quash the case against the Turkish bank.

item.title: Tell Us About Your Plans for a Longer Life
item.description: If you are saving money and have a plan for the future, we want to hear how it’s going and any advice you have for others.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: As Oil Prices Rise, the War With Iran Becomes a Worldwide Economic Hazard
item.description: President Trump insists the conflict will be brief, but world leaders are preparing for severe economic blowback.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm, Colby Smith and Alan Rappeport
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has repeatedly said that the war he commenced with Iran would be short-lived, rendering it unnecessary for the U.S. government to mount a major economic response.

item.title: Talent Agent Ari Emanuel Launches His Own Podcast, ‘Rushmore’
item.description: Hollywood’s archetypical agent has been showing a very different side of himself as a host of a video podcast called “Rushmore.”
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: Colman McCarthy, 87, Journalist Who Waged Peace in the Classroom, Dies
item.description: For decades, he wrote a syndicated column in The Washington Post promoting nonviolence. That became the subject of a course he taught for nearly 40 years.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald
item.media:description: Colman McCarthy in 2002. The newspaper columnist founded the nonprofit Center for Teaching Peace in Washington. “He was a steady and relentless force for good,” the singer Joan Baez said.

item.title: G7 Countries Hold Off on Releasing Emergency Oil Supplies
item.description: Finance ministers for the seven industrialized countries met on Monday and said they would consider releasing oil from reserves but were not ready to do so now.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Aurelien Breeden
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: Smoke rose from a refinery operated by Bapco Energies, Bahrain’s state-owned energy company, on Monday. Global oil prices have spiked since the U.S.-led war with Iran began.

item.title: An Amish Avatar and an A.I. Monk Are Pitching Supplements on Social Media
item.description: Influencers have long hawked supplements on platforms like Instagram and TikTok. Increasingly, the influencers are fake.
item.dc:creator: Ken Bensinger, Tiffany Hsu and Qilai Shen
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times

item.title: Live Nation, Ticketmaster’s Owner, Settles Antitrust Case With Justice Dept.
item.description: The agreement, which requires a judicial sign-off, would avoid a breakup of the concert giant. But states that joined the suit object to the terms.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario, David McCabe and Olivia Bensimon
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The tentative settlement ends a case that had bipartisan political support in Washington.

item.title: Countries in Asia Try to Contain the Economic Fallout of the Mideast Conflict
item.description: Across Asia, where countries are highly exposed to rising oil and gas costs and tightening supply, governments are acting to mitigate economic harm.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Krolik, Choe Sang-Hun, Sui-Lee Wee, Meaghan Tobin and Damien Cave
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow passage between Iran and Oman. Historically 20 million barrels pass through it each day, most of it bound for Asia.

item.title: Investors and Trump Face an Iran Dilemma
item.description: A spike in oil prices has roiled stocks and bonds again and threatens to accelerate inflation — adding pressure on the White House.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount, Grady McGregor and Jessica Testa
item.media:description: A big rise in oil prices has set off another global sell-off in stocks and bonds.

item.title: ‘The Old Game Is Back’: Oil Is a Potent Geopolitical Tool Again
item.description: The war in the Middle East has sent prices soaring, showing how much the world continues to depend on reliable supplies of oil and gas.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil tanker off the coast of El Segundo, Calif., last week. The United States is the world’s biggest producer of oil and natural gas but is still feeling the effects of the war in the Middle East.

item.title: A $1,000 Dog Grooming Session? The Wellness Industry Is Booming.
item.description: Pet owners are extending their health beliefs and practices to their furry friends.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Giang and Sophia June
item.media:credit: Amy Lombard for The New York Times
item.media:description: Vivian is grooming her dog, Porcini, a 2-year-old Norwich terrier, who requires a technique called hand stripping that not many groomers can do.

item.title: Consumers Paid Tariffs on Overseas Items. Now They Want a Refund.
item.description: The Trump administration has yet to announce a process to return fees paid by companies and shoppers for tariffs now deemed illegal.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.media:credit: Lauren O'Neil for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dr. Andrew Angel paid a tariff on a pendant he bought from an eBay seller in Japan as a gift for his wife, Dr. Irina Angel.

item.title: The Bank Trump Is Relying On for Rare-Earth Minerals
item.description: The Export-Import Bank is providing a $10 billion loan to Project Vault, an initiative to stockpile critical minerals. The project is the administration’s latest effort to reduce reliance on China.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: Office of the United States Trade Representative

item.title: How to Prepare for Your First River Cruise
item.description: As cruises gain popularity among travelers, here’s what to know before setting sail down a river.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy

item.title: U.S. Gasoline Prices, Rising Again, Hit $3.48 a Gallon
item.description: Interruptions in oil supplies in the Middle East, source of much of the world’s energy, are trickling down to what American drivers pay when they fill up.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A gas station in Washington, D.C., on Thursday.

item.title: The Former Coal Miner in the Middle of the A.I. Data Center Boom
item.description: Josh Payne, the chief executive of Nscale, raised $2 billion from investors. Sheryl Sandberg, the former Facebook executive, is joining his board.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano
item.media:credit: Charlotte Hadden for The New York Times

item.title: Stocks Rebound After Trump Says Iran War Is ‘Very Complete’
item.description: The S&P 500 ended slightly higher on Monday as investors interpreted President Trump’s comments as a signal that the conflict could end soon.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison, River Akira Davis, Meaghan Tobin and Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: For China, Billions of Dollars Are at Risk From a Widening War
item.description: The country found a home in the Middle East for its investments and growing markets for steel, electric vehicles and solar panels. Those are now at stake.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Beilun Port in China. The Middle East became even more important to China after China’s trade rivalry with the United States escalated.

item.title: Oil Prices Spike to Over $110 a Barrel, Highest Since Pandemic
item.description: The jump was a sign of growing concern that the war in the Middle East will take a toll on energy supplies.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The price of a gallon of regular gasoline has climbed about 16 percent since the United States and Israel attacked Iran on Feb. 28.

item.title: Have a Montana License Plate in California? Officials Have an Eye on You.
item.description: Since 2018, Californians have bought luxury vehicles worth over $20 million in sales-tax-free Montana, exploiting a loophole that avoided millions of dollars in levies, an investigation revealed.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Office of the Attorney General of California
item.media:description: The California Attorney General’s Office said that 14 people arranged tax-free sales of vehicles, including this $1.5 million Porsche 918 Spyder, by making the purchases in Montana.

item.title: Energy Secretary Says Tankers Could Start Moving Soon
item.description: “We’re not too long, I think, before you will see more regular resumption of ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz,” Chris Wright said on CNN on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Tankers anchored in Muscat, Oman. Chris Wright, the energy secretary, said it would take “a few weeks” at most for normal numbers of tankers to return to the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: ‘Hoppers’ Ends Pixar’s Box Office Cold Streak for Originals
item.description: The animated film was No. 1 at the domestic box office, with about $46 million in ticket sales. On the downside, “The Bride!” appeared dead on arrival.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: 24 Hours With Nike’s C.E.O. as He Races to Win Back the Sports World
item.description: Elliott Hill is traveling the globe to meet with leagues, teams and athletes to rebuild connections and spur a turnaround for the world’s largest athletic-wear company.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: François Ollivier for The New York Times

item.title: A Wink, a Nod or a Duck: The Secrets Behind Car Owners’ Secret Handshakes
item.description: It’s more than the Jeep Wave: Miatas can wink with their pop-up headlights, and Porsche owners have their own waves, but only for the right cars.
item.dc:creator: Roy Furchgott and Kelsey McClellan
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times

item.title: A Crypto Coin Is Gobbling Up U.S. Treasuries
item.description: A new generation of cryptocurrency, pegged to the dollar, is growing rapidly, promising faster payments and potentially lower interest rates. But regulators and bankers warn of risks.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
